pages.full_text: to 1790. They have frescoed walls & posture figures, life size. The chapels are simple & beautiful & set in the grounds in a pleasing way. It's calm
pages.notes: Page contains a large glued-in colour photograph of what appears to be a garden courtyard or cloister, and a smaller photograph of a painted artwork (figure study). The handwritten text is continuation from a previous page describing chapels built/decorated up to 1790. No handwritten page number visible.

pages.full_text:
Afterwards we walked down to the village & had [?pnjjos] in the square.

__Wednesday__
We spend the morning in camp. My tummy's bad, so in the afternoon Simon goes off on a train trip on his own.

I travelled 47 km north from St. [?Gialgio] to Domodossola which had an old centre dating from the Middle Ages. The streets still showed the pattern of its five walls.

It acquired a new life when Napoleon chose the Simplon pass as a main route from France to Italy, and yet again [decoration] with the building [of]
photo_captions: Città di Domodossola / Assessorato alla Cultura / Apre i suoi Musei
pages.notes: Handwritten page number '18' in top-right corner. 'pnjjos' is unclear — could be a local food item, possibly 'pizzas' or a phonetic spelling; context is a village square in northern Italy near Domodossola. 'St. Gialgio' likely refers to San Giorgio (a locality near Domodossola in Piedmont, Italy); could also read 'Gialgio' as 'Giorgio'. Page contains a glued-in printed promotional card for 'Città di Domodossola / Assessorato alla Cultura / Apre i suoi Musei' with a historical illustration of the town. Text cuts off at bottom mid-sentence ('with the building [of]'). The '[decoration]' represents a decorative leaf/grain motif printed in the diary. No year is stated on the page itself; the mention of Domodossola and the Simplon pass in Italy suggests a European trip within the 2003–2019 range of this volume, but cannot be more precise without surrounding pages.
